Front: What was the role of the Privy Council under Elizabeth I?
Back: To advise the monarch and oversee administration of government

Front: Who were Elizabeth I's most important ministers?
Back: William Cecil Lord Burghley and Francis Walsingham

Front: Why was William Cecil important?
Back: He was Elizabeth's chief advisor and managed government policy

Front: Why was Francis Walsingham important?
Back: He organised an extensive intelligence and spy network

Front: What was the purpose of Elizabeth's spy network?
Back: To uncover Catholic plots against the queen

Front: What was the Privy Chamber?
Back: A group of trusted attendants close to the monarch

Front: What was the role of Parliament in Elizabethan England?
Back: To pass laws and grant taxation

Front: Why did Elizabeth try to limit Parliamentary debate?
Back: To maintain royal authority and avoid challenges

Front: What issue caused tension between Elizabeth and Parliament?
Back: The succession question

Front: Why was the succession question sensitive?
Back: Elizabeth refused to name an heir

Front: What were Puritans?
Back: Protestants who wanted further reform of the Church of England

Front: Why did Elizabeth oppose Puritan reforms?
Back: She wanted religious stability and uniformity

Front: What were recusants?
Back: Catholics who refused to attend Anglican services

Front: What fines were imposed on recusants?
Back: Financial penalties for missing church services

Front: What was the Northern Rebellion 1569?
Back: A Catholic uprising aiming to replace Elizabeth with Mary Queen of Scots

Front: Why did the Northern Rebellion fail?
Back: Lack of widespread support and government suppression

Front: Who led the Northern Rebellion?
Back: The Earls of Northumberland and Westmorland

Front: What was the Ridolfi Plot?
Back: A Catholic conspiracy to replace Elizabeth with Mary Queen of Scots

Front: When did the Ridolfi Plot occur?
What was the Throckmorton Plot?
Back: A Catholic plot involving Spanish invasion plans

Front: When did the Throckmorton Plot occur?
What was the Babington Plot?
Back: A Catholic conspiracy to assassinate Elizabeth

Front: Why was the Babington Plot significant?
Back: It led to the execution of Mary Queen of Scots

Front: What role did Walsingham play in the Babington Plot?
Back: He uncovered the conspiracy through intercepted letters

Front: Why was Mary Queen of Scots considered a threat?
Back: She had a strong Catholic claim to the English throne

Front: When was Mary Queen of Scots executed?
Why did Elizabeth hesitate to execute Mary?
Back: She feared international backlash and precedent

Front: Why did Philip II of Spain plan to invade England?
Back: Religious conflict and English support for Dutch rebels

Front: What was the Spanish Armada?
Back: A large Spanish fleet sent to invade England in 1588

Front: What was Spain's strategy for the Armada?
Back: To escort an invasion army from the Netherlands to England

Front: Why did the Armada struggle in the English Channel?
Back: English ships were faster and used long range guns

Front: What role did fire ships play?
Back: They disrupted the Spanish formation

Front: What natural factors contributed to the Armada's defeat?
Back: Storms and difficult navigation

Front: Why was the Armada defeat significant?
Back: It strengthened English national confidence

Front: What was the role of the Royal Navy?
Back: To defend England and challenge Spanish power

Front: What economic problem affected Elizabethan England?
Back: Inflation

Front: Why did inflation increase?
Back: Population growth and rising food demand

Front: How did population growth affect society?
Back: It increased poverty and pressure on resources

Front: What was enclosure?
Back: Landowners fencing common land for private farming

Front: Why did enclosure cause unrest?
Back: It displaced rural workers

Front: What were the Poor Laws?
Back: Laws designed to help the poor and control vagrancy

Front: When were the Elizabethan Poor Laws introduced?
What was the aim of the Poor Laws?
Back: To organise local responsibility for poverty relief

Front: Who administered the Poor Laws?
Back: Local parish officials

Front: What were vagrants?
Back: People wandering without work

Front: Why were vagrants feared?
Back: They were associated with crime and disorder

Front: What was the role of Justices of the Peace?
Back: To maintain law and order locally

Front: How did Elizabeth strengthen local government?
Back: By relying on local gentry as officials

Front: What was patronage in Elizabethan politics?
Back: Granting positions or rewards to supporters

Front: Why was patronage important?
Back: It secured loyalty to the crown

Front: What was faction in Elizabeth's court?
Back: Rival groups competing for influence

Front: Why did Elizabeth manage factions carefully?
Back: To prevent ministers gaining too much power

Front: What was the significance of the Treaty of Nonsuch?
Back: England supported Dutch rebels against Spain

Front: When was the Treaty of Nonsuch signed?
Why did England support the Dutch?
Back: To weaken Spanish power

Front: What was the significance of English privateers?
Back: They attacked Spanish treasure fleets

Front: Who was Francis Drake?
Back: An English privateer and naval commander

Front: Why was Drake famous?
Back: He circumnavigated the globe and fought Spain

Front: What was the significance of the defeat of the Armada?
Back: It marked a turning point in the Anglo Spanish conflict

Front: What was the Earl of Essex rebellion?
Back: A failed uprising against Elizabeth in 1601

Front: Why did Essex rebel?
Back: He lost political influence and sought power

Front: Why did the Essex rebellion fail?
Back: Lack of support and swift suppression

Front: What was the significance of Elizabeth's reign?
Back: It brought relative stability after religious turmoil

Front: Why is Elizabeth considered a successful monarch?
Back: She maintained political stability and avoided major domestic crises

Front: What challenges faced Elizabeth late in her reign?
Back: Economic hardship factional conflict and succession uncertainty

Front: Why was succession still unresolved in 1603?
Back: Elizabeth never married or named an heir

Front: Who succeeded Elizabeth I?
Back: James VI of Scotland

Front: Why was James chosen as successor?
Back: He was the closest Protestant claimant

Front: What was the significance of the Tudor dynasty?
Back: It strengthened monarchy and reshaped religion in England

Front: What was the overall impact of Tudor government?
Back: Centralised authority and expanded royal administration
